title: Judge halts Paramount's $111B purchase of Warner Bros. in win for US states
gist: A federal judge granted a 14-day temporary restraining order blocking Paramount Skydance's $111B acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery, finding the states likely to prove the merger substantially lessens competition (27% theatrical share, presumption of illegality via HHI), with a preliminary injunction hearing set for August 3.

insights.claim: A group of 12 states led by California blocked—via emergency TRO—the $111 billion Paramount-Warner Bros. Discovery merger even though the deal had already been approved by the Trump administration.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: State AG coalitions can now stall federally-blessed megadeals, adding an unpriced regulatory tail risk to merger-arb spreads in media consolidation.
insights.locator: A group of 12 states led by California sued the companies

insights.claim: The merger would combine two of the five major Hollywood movie studios and two of the five major owners of basic cable TV channels, the states argued.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The 5-to-4 reduction in both film and cable-channel ownership frames the structural harm case that will drive the outcome.
insights.locator: The states say the merger will eliminate competition

insights.claim: The merged company would hold a 27% share of the wide-release theatrical distribution market, and the judge noted 30% is not the lowest share that can pose an antitrust threat.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Sub-30% share being treated as presumptively illegal lowers the bar for future media/tech merger challenges—raises deal risk broadly.
insights.locator: The merged company is expected to have a 27 percent share

insights.claim: The judge found the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index increase for the deal exceeds the level needed to show enhanced market power, letting plaintiffs skip 'elaborate proof' of anticompetitive effects to obtain relief.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Reliance on HHI-based structural presumption means concentration math alone can halt a deal—predictable, quantifiable screening for arbitrageurs.
insights.quote: the HHI increase for Paramount/WBD exceeds the level necessary to show the merger is likely to enhance market power.
insights.locator: The judge also pointed to a sharp rise in concentration

insights.claim: The defendants conceded they will not begin to incur carrying costs for a delayed merger until the end of September 2026, which the court used to conclude they suffer no near-term harm from an injunction.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The end-September carrying-cost date is the practical clock on how hard the companies will fight for speed; watch for urgency after that.
insights.quote: they concede that they will not begin to incur carrying costs for a delayed merger until the end of September 2026
insights.locator: “Defendants will suffer no apparent harm in the near term

insights.claim: A preliminary-injunction hearing is set for August 3, and Paramount can appeal to the 9th Circuit and is likely to do so if the merger remains barred until trial.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: August 3 is the next catalyst date for the deal spread; a preliminary injunction would push resolution to a full trial.
insights.locator: Martínez-Olguín set a schedule for the sides to submit briefs
